examples.title: FIFO Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of First-In-First-Out page replacement algorithm
examples.code:
def fifo_page_replacement(reference_string, num_frames):
    frames = []
    page_faults = 0
    queue = []  # To track insertion order
    
    for page in reference_string:
        if page not in frames:
            page_faults += 1
            if len(frames) < num_frames:
                frames.append(page)
                queue.append(page)
            else:
                # Replace oldest page (FIFO)
                victim = queue.pop(0)
                frames.remove(victim)
                frames.append(page)
                queue.append(page)
        print(f"Page {page}: Frames={frames}, Fault={page_faults}")
    
    return page_faults

# Example usage
reference = [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(f"Total Page Faults (FIFO): {fifo_page_replacement(reference, 3)}")
examples.explanation: This code simulates FIFO page replacement. When a page fault occurs and all frames are full, the page that entered memory first is replaced. A queue maintains the order of page arrivals.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × f) where n = reference string length, f = number of frames
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) for storing frames and queue

examples.title: LRU Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of Least Recently Used page replacement algorithm
examples.code:
def lru_page_replacement(reference_string, num_frames):
    frames = []
    page_faults = 0
    # Dictionary to store last used time for each page
    last_used = {}
    time_counter = 0
    
    for page in reference_string:
        time_counter += 1
        
        if page not in frames:
            page_faults += 1
            if len(frames) < num_frames:
                frames.append(page)
            else:
                # Find LRU page (least recent access)
                lru_page = min(frames, key=lambda p: last_used.get(p, 0))
                frames.remove(lru_page)
                frames.append(page)
        
        last_used[page] = time_counter
        print(f"Page {page}: Frames={frames}, Fault={page_faults}")
    
    return page_faults

# Example usage
reference = [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(f"Total Page Faults (LRU): {lru_page_replacement(reference, 3)}")
examples.explanation: LRU replaces the page that was not used for the longest time. This implementation uses a time counter to track the last access time of each page in memory.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × f) where n = reference string length, f = number of frames
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) for frames plus O(f) for tracking last used times

examples.title: TLB Simulation
examples.description: Simulation of Translation Lookaside Buffer operations in C
examples.explanation: This C program simulates TLB operations including lookup, insertion with FIFO replacement, and address translation. It demonstrates the two-level process: first check TLB, then page table if TLB miss occurs.
examples.timeComplexity: TLB lookup O(TLB_SIZE), page table access O(1)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(TLB_SIZE) for TLB cache plus O(PAGE_TABLE_SIZE) for page table

examples.title: Clock Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of Second Chance (Clock) page replacement algorithm
examples.code:
class ClockAlgorithm:
    def __init__(self, num_frames):
        self.num_frames = num_frames
        self.frames = []
        self.reference_bits = []  # Use bits for each frame
        self.pointer = 0  # Clock hand position
        self.page_faults = 0
    
    def replace_page(self, page):
        # Keep rotating until we find a page with reference bit = 0
        while True:
            if self.reference_bits[self.pointer] == 0:
                # Replace this page
                self.frames[self.pointer] = page
                self.reference_bits[self.pointer] = 1  # Set reference bit
                self.pointer = (self.pointer + 1) % self.num_frames
                return
            else:
                # Give second chance: clear reference bit
                self.reference_bits[self.pointer] = 0
                self.pointer = (self.pointer + 1) % self.num_frames
    
    def access_page(self, page):
        if page in self.frames:
            # Page hit - set reference bit to 1
            idx = self.frames.index(page)
            self.reference_bits[idx] = 1
            print(f"Page {page}: HIT - Frames: {self.frames}")
        else:
            # Page fault
            self.page_faults += 1
            if len(self.frames) < self.num_frames:
                self.frames.append(page)
                self.reference_bits.append(1)
            else:
                self.replace_page(page)
            print(f"Page {page}: FAULT - Frames: {self.frames}")
    
    def run(self, reference_string):
        for page in reference_string:
            self.access_page(page)
        return self.page_faults

# Example usage
clock = ClockAlgorithm(3)
reference = [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
total_faults = clock.run(reference)
print(f"\nTotal Page Faults: {total_faults}")
examples.explanation: The Clock algorithm uses a circular buffer and reference bits. It gives pages a second chance if recently used (reference bit = 1). This approach approximates LRU while being implementable efficiently.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × c) where n = reference length, c = number of frames (worst case may scan all frames)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) for frames and reference bits
